[149:53]are fine with that.

[149:55]Not only do they want that in Gaza, but they also want to expand this war in Gaza to Lebanon,

[150:02]to Yemen, and ultimately to Iran and to destroy Iran's nuclear program and eventually topple

[150:09]The Trump administration is opposed on all counts.

[150:13]They do not necessarily want Israel to invade Gaza.

[150:17]And if they do, they want them to do it quickly and to wrap it up so they can make their deal

[150:22]with Saudi Arabia.

[150:24]The Trump government does not want a war with Iran.

[150:27]Maybe some elements in the Trump government do.

[150:32]And elements inside the government like, I think, Vance and Tulsi Gabbard and maybe even Pete Hegzeff, Steve Wittkoff, they do not want a protracted war or to bomb Iran at all.

[150:45]So there was, from the very beginning, a significant divergence and we saw that very quickly.

[150:51]Netanyahu was unwilling to even discuss with Hamas through mediators how they were going

[150:58]to implement the second phase of that three phase deal because they had no intention of

[151:04]reaching it.

[151:10]was back on all over again.

[151:17]That's why from the very beginning, if you recall, the Trump government was chastised

[151:23]by Israel because Trump was hosting his own bilateral negotiations with Hamas, without

[151:31]Israel as a mediator.

[152:00]I, I apologize.

[152:03]So when I say that there is rumors of a divergence, it's because there has been a real divergence

[152:10]for a very long time.

[152:11]Then there was the initiation of negotiations with Iran to begin with.

[152:29]This was a week after liberation day, I think April 9th or April 8th, Netanyahu visited Washington

[153:37]and might pave the way for a short-term interim agreement

[154:32]launched over 1,100 airstrikes against them.

[156:01]So people have said there's a few things that the United States has diverged from Israel

[156:05]on.

[156:06]There's no fewer than four or five things to date.

[156:11]It's May.

[156:12]Trump is in government for just over a hundred days.

[156:16]And there are four, maybe five, depending on how you count them, areas where the US government,

[156:22]the Trump government is completely diverged from Israel. And it's not only diverged from them

[156:27]in terms of policy, we're going in a different direction, but we're not even keeping them in

[156:55]The CIA the State Department Central Command in the US military

[156:59]There is no daylight, but we effectively are the same entity

[157:43]not even including the firing of Mike Waltz, which had a lot to do with this, why in the

[157:56]mainstream media outlets that the Trump administration has had a major falling out with Benjamin

[158:02]Netanyahu?

[159:02]And ultimately, after all that was done, Trump was going to facilitate the expulsion of two

[159:07]million Palestinians to various Muslim majority countries, and then top it all off by bombing

[159:22]50 years, even longer than that, but ever since the dawn of like Kud Party's dominance

[159:27]in Israel, and that is total domination of the Middle East strategically, that Israel

[159:34]would be the only hegemon in the entire region with zero challengers.

[159:40]That's how Netanyahu wanted this to go down.

[159:43]Trump has a very different picture of the Middle East.

[159:50]that Netanyahu would wind down the war in Gaza very quickly.

[159:54]He'd be able to get Netanyahu to do that, that they would finish the job very quickly.

[160:12]He'd be able to bring Saudi Arabia and Israel together.

[177:44]held their fourth round of negotiations

[177:47]in week five of negotiations with Iran.

[177:51]They met this Saturday.

[177:52]This is the second time

[177:54]where the negotiations were actually technical.

[177:58]The first two meetings and the first two weeks were very general. They were indirect. The

[178:03]two sides did not meet except for to shake hands at the very end. And they just talked

[178:08]very generally about what the negotiations would be about themselves. They were negotiations

[178:14]about negotiations in the very beginning. The discussion was missiles, nukes, weapons,

[178:26]And so the first meeting was, what are these negotiations really gonna be about?

[178:31]Will they include all those topics, all those items or just the nuclear program?

[178:37]After the second meeting, they realized it was only going to include the nuclear program,

[178:43]not necessarily their ballistic missile program, excuse me, not their support for the Houthis

[178:54]The third talks were so difficult, they canceled the fourth round.

[178:59]They canceled them and then they put them back on the schedule.

[179:02]That's because the third round of talks was the first time they actually talked about what a deal would include.

[179:09]And since that meeting, the U.S. has gone back and forth and back and forth about what we actually want out of the negotiations.

[179:17]This time again, we have the fourth round in the fifth week after a break in the middle. Like I said last week, they held no negotiations

[179:26]Once again this week we held talks and both sides said they're very optimistic. It was very positive

[179:32]But both sides also said it's very tough

[179:35]And we're basically at an impasse and the question in Iran. It's the same question in Saudi Arabia

[179:42]The question is whether Iran with their civilian nuclear program is going to be able to enrich

[179:48]uranium or if they'll have to dismantle their entire nuclear infrastructure.

[179:53]The United States still doesn't have a position.
